attributes.stem:
A 38-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about changes in her phantom limb pain. She underwent a below-knee amputation of her right leg following a motor vehicle accident 9 months ago. She describes constant burning pain and intermittent shooting sensations in her phantom foot, which have been relatively stable and managed with gabapentin and psychological strategies. Over the past three weeks, she reports new sharp, electric shock-like pain that radiates from her stump. She has no fever or other systemic symptoms. Her medical history includes post-traumatic stress disorder and depression. Her medications include gabapentin, sertraline, and paracetamol.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her stump is well-healed but tender to palpation at the distal end. There is no erythema, swelling, or increased local temperature.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the stump
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a change in her established phantom limb pain pattern, raising concern for stump pathology such as neuroma formation or soft tissue changes. The new electric shock-like pain radiating from the stump is particularly suggestive of neuroma development.

Ultrasound of the stump is the most appropriate first-line investigation as it is non-invasive, readily available, and can effectively identify soft tissue abnormalities including neuromas, which appear as hypoechoic masses in continuity with the affected nerve. It can also detect other complications such as bursitis or soft tissue inflammation.

Magnetic resonance imaging provides detailed soft tissue imaging and can successfully identify neuroma's, but are not necessary as a first-line investigation. It would be more appropriate if ultrasound findings were equivocal or if surgical planning was required.

X-ray of the stump would be appropriate if bone pathology was suspected, such as heterotopic ossification or osteomyelitis, but the clinical presentation does not suggest these conditions.

Computed tomography offers limited advantage over simpler imaging modalities for soft tissue pathology in this context and involves unnecessary radiation exposure.

Nerve conduction studies may be helpful in characterising nerve dysfunction but would not be the first-line investigation when structural pathology is suspected. They are more useful in diagnosing compression neuropathies or assessing the extent of nerve damage.

The management of phantom limb pain requires a comprehensive approach including medication optimisation, psychological support, and physical therapy. However, when established phantom pain patterns change, careful investigation for underlying pathology is essential before attributing symptoms to psychological factors.
learningPoints: Changes in phantom limb pain characteristics warrant investigation to exclude stump pathology or neuroma formation.
Clinical examination and ultrasound are first-line investigations for new or changed phantom limb pain.
Psychological assessment is an important component of phantom limb pain management but should not delay investigation of new symptoms.
references: Atar MÖ, Demir Y, Kamacı GK, Korkmaz N, Aslan SG, Aydemir K. Neuroma prevalence and neuroma-associated factors in patients with traumatic lower extremity amputation. Gulhane Med J. 2022 Mar;64(1):54-59. doi:10.4274/gulhane.galenos.2021.32932. Available online at ; https://gulhanemedj.org/articles/neuroma-prevalence-and-neuroma-associated-factors-in-patients-with-traumatic-lower-extremity-amputation/doi/gulhane.galenos.2021.32932

Erlenwein J, Diers M, Ernst J, Schulz F, Petzke F. Clinical updates on phantom limb pain. Pain reports. 2021 Apr 1;6(1):e888. Available online at ; https://journals.lww.com/painrpts/fulltext/2021/01000/Clinical_updates_on_phantom_limb_pain.7.aspx/1000
